data.title: If You Invested $100 In Texas Instruments Stock 15 Years Ago, You Would Have This Much Today
data.description:
Texas Instruments (NASDAQ:TXN) has outperformed the market over the past 15 years by 1.14% on an annualized basis producing an average annual return of 12.24%. Currently, Texas Instruments has a market capitalization of $177.14 billion.

Buying $100 In TXN: If an investor had bought $100 of TXN stock 15 years ago, it would be worth $559.64 today based on a price of $195.30 for TXN at the time of writing.

Texas Instruments's Performance Over Last 15 Years

Finally -- what's the point of all this? The key insight to take from this article is to note how much of a difference compounded returns can make in your cash growth over a period of time.

data.title: Netflix Hikes Prices For All Plans As Content Spending Surges
data.description:
• NFLX stock is moving. See the chart and pice action here.

Netflix’s New Pricing

The ad-supported plan now costs $8.99, up from $7.99, while the standard plan jumps to $19.99 and the premium tier rises to $26.99.

Fees for "extra member" add-ons have also increased — $6.99 per additional user on ad-supported plans (previously $5.99) and $9.99 for ad-free accounts (up from $8.99).

Content Spending Grows

Netflix justified the price hike by pointing to its growing slate of content and new ventures such as live shows and video podcasts.

The streamer plans to pour $20 billion into programming this year, roughly $2 billion more than in 2025, as it aims to boost both subscribers and ad revenue.

The company previously forecast 2026 revenue between $50.7 billion and $51.7 billion, noting that higher membership fees and a near-doubling of ad income would drive growth.

The move follows similar hikes by other major streamers seeking to balance rising content costs with profitability pressures.

NFLX Price Action

Netflix shares are up 0.38% at $93.67 in after-hours trading on Thursday, following a 1.13% climb during the regular session, according to Benzinga Pro.

Photo: Shutterstock

data.title: Unity Software Stock Soars After The Close: Here's Why
data.description:
• Unity Software stock is among today’s strongest performers. Why is U stock rising?

Unity Reports Preliminary Q1 Results

Unity announced Thursday that it expects first-quarter results to come in above prior guidance. The company sees first-quarter revenue in the range of $505 million to $508 million versus prior guidance of $480 million to $490 million.

Unity also guided for first-quarter adjusted EBITDA of $130 million to $135 million versus prior guidance of $105 million to $110 million.

The strong performance in the quarter was driven by Unity Vector, which is expected to grow 15% sequentially in the first quarter.

Unity also announced that it will be sunsetting its ironSource Ads Network, effective April 30. The company expects the change to drive faster revenue growth and higher adjusted EBTIDA.

“Unity Vector continues to deliver robust growth each quarter, driving results meaningfully above our guidance. Today’s actions will accelerate Vector’s impact on our business, enhancing both revenue growth and profitability,” said Matt Bromberg, president and CEO of Unity.

U Shares Surge After The Close

U Price Action: Unity Software shares were up 15% in after-hours, trading at $19.70 at the time of publication on Thursday, according to Benzinga Pro.

Image: Shutterstock.com

data.title: Wife Tells Suze Orman Her Husband Wants To Invest $100K In His Fintech Employer, Even Though It's In The Red —'That's How Families Get Obliterated'
data.description:
Some couples argue over takeout. Others argue over whether to tie their entire financial life to one company. This one lands firmly in the second category—and it's the kind of decision that can ripple far beyond a single investment.

Don't Miss:

Victoria already felt uneasy. The couple had previously loaned the company $50,000 at 8%, and that loan is now being converted into stock. To her, it sounded like doubling down on a shaky bet. To him, it likely looked like belief in the company's future. She asked the question many partners quietly wrestle with: is this smart, or is this risk getting out of hand?

When Belief Starts To Blur Into Risk

Orman didn't hesitate to zoom out. Early-stage fintech companies, she said, often operate at a loss for years, and many never make it to a public offering. Along the way, repeated funding rounds can dilute existing shareholders, shrinking the value of what employees think they own.

Then she added a layer that goes beyond spreadsheets. "Employees often overestimate the value of their stock," she said. "There's also an ego thing involved."

That line cuts to the psychology behind decisions like this. When someone works inside a company, the belief in its success can feel personal. It's not just an investment—it's a reflection of their work, their judgment, even their identity. That makes it harder to step back and assess the risk clearly.

Trending: Think you're saving enough for your kids? You might be dangerously off — see why

One Company, One paycheck, One big exposure

What pushed this scenario into dangerous territory, in Orman's view, was how much of the couple's life was already tied to the same place.

The husband's salary comes from the company. His career trajectory depends on it. Their health insurance is tied to it. Bonuses, stability, future growth—it all runs through that single employer. Add in $150,000 of their own money at risk, and the exposure starts to stack in a way that leaves little room for error.

Orman named it directly: "This is called double concentration risk."

If the company struggles or fails, the fallout doesn't hit just one area. It hits everything at once—income, benefits, and savings. That's not diversification. That's dependency.

The line that says it all

Orman didn't soften the conclusion. "I wouldn't do this if I were you," she said. Then she summed up the stakes in a way that's hard to ignore: "That's how families get obliterated."

It's a blunt way of describing a quiet reality. Big financial setbacks rarely come from one bad move in isolation. They tend to come from layers of risk piling up in the same place.

In this case, the husband's confidence in his company may be genuine. But from a household perspective, the question isn't just whether the company succeeds. It's what happens if it doesn't—and how much of the family's financial life is riding on that single outcome.

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: Blink Charging Stock Stalls After Reporting Q4 Results
data.description:
• Blink Charging stock is showing notable weakness. Why is BLNK stock falling?

Blink Q4 Highlights

Blink reported fourth-quarter revenue of $27.04 million, missing analyst estimates of $28.56 million. The company reported an adjusted loss of 11 cents per share, in line with analyst estimates, according to Benzinga Pro.

Product revenue totaled $11 million in the quarter, down from $17.2 million in the comparable quarter. Meanwhile, services revenue increased 62% year-over-year to $14.7 million. Other revenue came in at $1.3 million, down from $1.8 million.

Blink ended the quarter with $39.6 million in total cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities. The company noted that it has reduced cash burn by 85% since the first quarter to approximately $2 million per quarter.

“2025 was defined by our disciplined execution and strengthening the core of our business. We streamlined operations and our cost structure, improved margins and grew repeatable and recurring service revenue, putting Blink on a resilient and scalable path,” said Mike Battaglia, president and CEO of Blink Charging.

“Blink is now operating as a faster, leaner organization with a durable long-term direction, and we will continue executing with that same focus as we expand our owner-operated DC fast charging network in the most lucrative markets.”

Blink expects full-year 2026 revenue to be in the range of $105 million to $115 million, with gross margins of approximately 35%. The company also anticipates “significantly reduced adjusted EBITDA losses” compared to prior periods. 

Blink executives will further discuss the quarter on an earnings call scheduled for 4:30 p.m. ET.

Blink Shares Hold Steady After The Bell

BLNK Price Action: Blink shares were down 1.64% in after-hours Thursday, trading at approximately 60 cents at the time of publication, according to Benzinga Pro.

Image: Shutterstock.com
